<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[sn:commons:Category:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[de:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[en:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[es:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[fa:سانجوسانگن-دو]][[fi:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[fr:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[gan:三十三間堂]][[hu:Szandzsúszangen-dó]][[id:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[it:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[ja:三十三間堂]][[my:ဆန်းဂျူးဆန်းဂန်းဒိုး]][[pl:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[pt:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[ru:Сандзюсангэн-до]][[th:ซันจูซังเก็ง-โด]][[tr:Sanjūsangen-dō]][[uk:Храм Сандзюсанґен]][[zh:三十三間堂]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Sanjusangendo temple01s1408.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}}|prefecture={{ill|Higashiyama-ku|WD=Q1203862}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.987885|N|135.771713|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984}}; {{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|WD=Q281833}}|deity={{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q10904910}}|part_of={{ill|Myōhō-in|WD=Q10943054}}; {{ill|Q10380674|WD=Q10380674}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q653319]] Wikidata name: Sanjūsangen-dō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Sanjūsangen-dō | native_name = | image = Sanjusangendo 2022.jpg | image_size = 200px | alt = | caption = Sanjusangen-dō in 2022 | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 605-0941, Sanjusangendomawari, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] 675 | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = Thousand Armed [[Kannon]] ([[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']]) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] | year_completed = 1164 }}
{{Nihongo|'''Sanjūsangen-dō'''|三十三間堂||''Temple of thirty-three [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]]''}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect in the [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama district]] of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The temple was founded in 1164 by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. It is officially known as {{nihongo|''Rengeō-in''|蓮華王院||''hall of the Lotus King''}} and belongs to the [[Myōhō-in]] temple complex.
Sanjūsangen-dō is most famous for its massively long ''hondō'' (main hall) dating from 1266 ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) and designated a [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure of Japan]], and the collection of sculptures it houses, including 1001 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]], 28 standing attendants, a statue of [[Fūjin]] and a statue of [[Raijin]], and the principal image of the temple, a big seated statue of Thousand-armed Kannon, all of them designated [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|National Treasures in the Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category of sculptures]], most of them dating to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] to [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]s.
{{coord|34|59|16|N|135|46|18|E|display=title}}
== History == {{multiple image|perrow = 2 | image1 = Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png|width1=158 | image2 = Emperor Go-Shirakawa2.jpg|width2=158 | footer = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] }}
Sanjūsangen-dō was founded by the famous ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' and politician [[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118-1181) in 1164 for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].<ref name="Encyclopedia">{{cite web |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Sanjusangendo/ |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=[[World History Encyclopedia]] |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref><ref name="JapanGuide">{{cite web |url=https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3900.html |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=Japan Guide |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref> He built the temple in the emperor's own compound ''Hōjūji-dono'' in order to gain a noble title, that of Chancellor of the Realm, becoming the first ''samurai'' to do so. Go-Shirakawa's compound was around 1100 square meters in size, divided into ''Minamidono'' (the southern estate) and ''Kitadono'' (the northern estate). When Go-Shirakawa died in 1192, he was buried in the temple's east ''Hokkedō'' (hall of the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]]).<ref name="panels">Informative panels behind the main hall</ref>
The temple complex originally included several buildings other than the ''hondō'' ([[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]), including a ''gojūnotō'' (five-storied [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]]), a Kannondō (a hall of [[Kannon]]) and a Fudodō (a hall of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]).<ref name="panels" /> All of these buildings were completely destroyed in 1249 by a fire that broke out in the city.<ref name="Encyclopedia" /> The [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] (1220–1272) ordered the reconstruction of the ''hondō'', which began in 1251. The building was completed in 1266 and survives to the present day. From the original 1000 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]] dating from the temple's construction in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only 124 were saved from the fire. The Emperor also ordered 876 new Kannon statues to replace the lost ones. These were created by three groups of Buddhist sculptors, [[:en:Kei school|Kei school]] (Keiha), En school (Enpa) and In school (Inpa), during the course of 16 years.<ref name="panels"/>
A popular ''[[:en:Kyūjutsu|Kyūjutsu]]'' (archery) tournament known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'' ("passing arrow") was held at the west veranda of the temple for 255 years during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The contest originated in the late 16th century<ref>{{cite book |title=National Treasure Sanjūsangen-dō |location=[[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] |page=30 }}</ref> dating back to 1606 when a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named Asaoka Heibei is said to have shot 51 arrows in rapid succession down the length of the veranda.<ref>{{cite book |title=Lonely Planet Kyoto: City Guide |last=Rowthorn |first=Chris |year=2008 |publisher=Lonely Planet Publications |location=China |page=15 |isbn=9781740598453 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LR_Qj8x-iT4C&q=Lonely+Planet+Kyoto |access-date=2010-02-01}}</ref> In the beginning, archers shot arrows from the southern end of the veranda to the northern end where a [[:en:Curtain|Curtain]]-like ornament was erected as a [[:en:Bullseye (target)|target]]. The contest gained popularity during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and by the late 17th century competitions between participants from the [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū]] provinces were drawing big crowds.<ref name="panels" />
The duel between the famous warrior [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]] and Yoshioka Denshichirō, leader of the [[:en:Yoshioka-ryū|Yoshioka-ryū]], is popularly believed to have been fought just outside Sanjūsangen-dō in 1604.<ref name="panels" />
In the second Sunday of January, the temple has an event known as the ''Rite of the Willow'', where worshippers are touched on the head with a sacred willow branch to cure and prevent headaches, and a modern version of the ''Tōshiya'', the ''Festival of the Great Target'', is held on the west veranda, drawing roughly 2,000 participants from throughout Japan. Archers shoot arrows into targets approximately 50 - 100 centimeters in diameter and 60 meters (198 feet) away at the opposite end of the veranda. The festival was initially male-dominated, but in recent times it has shifted to being mainly women dressed in traditional [[:en:Kimono|kimonos]], with the target being 120 meters (394 feet) away.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="panels" /><ref name="JapanGuide" />

The temple also contains one thousand life-size statues of the Thousand Armed Kannon which stand on both the right and left sides of the main statue in 10 rows and 50 columns. Of these, 124 statues are from the original temple, rescued from the fire of 1249, while the remaining 876 statues were constructed in the 13th century. The statues are made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]] clad in gold leaf. The temple is 120 - meter long.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/kyoto/sanjusangen-do|title=Sanjusangen-do - Kyoto Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-08-16}}</ref> Around the 1000 Kannon statues stand 28 statues of guardian deities. There are also two famous statues of [[Fūjin]] and [[Raijin]].

Jarzombek and Vikramaditya Prakash, A Global History of Architecture, 2nd Edition, Wiley, {{ISBN|978-0470402573}}</ref>
Life-size statues of these deities are housed at Sanjūsangen-dō where they guard the principal statue of the 11 feet tall seated Senju Kannon. Chandra (1988), The Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara, {{ISBN|978-8170172475}}, Chapter 1</ref>

Cameras are not allowed inside the temple, but a photo book will be offered at the end of the guided tour that you can purchase.

{{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|WD=Q281833}}|deity={{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q10904910}}|part_of={{ill|Myōhō-in|WD=Q10943054}}; {{ill|Q10380674|WD=Q10380674}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q653319]] Wikidata name: Sanjūsangen-dō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Sanjūsangen-dō | native_name = | image = Sanjusangendo 2022.jpg | image_size = 200px | alt = | caption = Sanjusangen-dō in 2022 | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 605-0941, Sanjusangendomawari, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] 675 | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = Thousand Armed [[Kannon]] ([[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']]) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] | year_completed = 1164 }}
{{Nihongo|'''Sanjūsangen-dō'''|三十三間堂||''Temple of thirty-three [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]]''}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect in the [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama district]] of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The temple was founded in 1164 by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. It is officially known as {{nihongo|''Rengeō-in''|蓮華王院||''hall of the Lotus King''}} and belongs to the [[Myōhō-in]] temple complex.
Sanjūsangen-dō is most famous for its massively long ''hondō'' (main hall) dating from 1266 ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) and designated a [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure of Japan]], and the collection of sculptures it houses, including 1001 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]], 28 standing attendants, a statue of [[Fūjin]] and a statue of [[Raijin]], and the principal image of the temple, a big seated statue of Thousand-armed Kannon, all of them designated [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|National Treasures in the Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category of sculptures]], most of them dating to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] to [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]s.
{{coord|34|59|16|N|135|46|18|E|display=title}}
== History == {{multiple image|perrow = 2 | image1 = Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png|width1=158 | image2 = Emperor Go-Shirakawa2.jpg|width2=158 | footer = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] }}
Sanjūsangen-dō was founded by the famous ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' and politician [[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118-1181) in 1164 for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].<ref name="Encyclopedia">{{cite web |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Sanjusangendo/ |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=[[World History Encyclopedia]] |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref><ref name="JapanGuide">{{cite web |url=https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3900.html |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=Japan Guide |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref> He built the temple in the emperor's own compound ''Hōjūji-dono'' in order to gain a noble title, that of Chancellor of the Realm, becoming the first ''samurai'' to do so. Go-Shirakawa's compound was around 1100 square meters in size, divided into ''Minamidono'' (the southern estate) and ''Kitadono'' (the northern estate). When Go-Shirakawa died in 1192, he was buried in the temple's east ''Hokkedō'' (hall of the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]]).<ref name="panels">Informative panels behind the main hall</ref>
The temple complex originally included several buildings other than the ''hondō'' ([[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]), including a ''gojūnotō'' (five-storied [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]]), a Kannondō (a hall of [[Kannon]]) and a Fudodō (a hall of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]).<ref name="panels" /> All of these buildings were completely destroyed in 1249 by a fire that broke out in the city.<ref name="Encyclopedia" /> The [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] (1220–1272) ordered the reconstruction of the ''hondō'', which began in 1251. The building was completed in 1266 and survives to the present day. From the original 1000 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]] dating from the temple's construction in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only 124 were saved from the fire. The Emperor also ordered 876 new Kannon statues to replace the lost ones. These were created by three groups of Buddhist sculptors, [[:en:Kei school|Kei school]] (Keiha), En school (Enpa) and In school (Inpa), during the course of 16 years.<ref name="panels"/>
A popular ''[[:en:Kyūjutsu|Kyūjutsu]]'' (archery) tournament known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'' ("passing arrow") was held at the west veranda of the temple for 255 years during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The contest originated in the late 16th century<ref>{{cite book |title=National Treasure Sanjūsangen-dō |location=[[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] |page=30 }}</ref> dating back to 1606 when a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named Asaoka Heibei is said to have shot 51 arrows in rapid succession down the length of the veranda.<ref>{{cite book |title=Lonely Planet Kyoto: City Guide |last=Rowthorn |first=Chris |year=2008 |publisher=Lonely Planet Publications |location=China |page=15 |isbn=9781740598453 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LR_Qj8x-iT4C&q=Lonely+Planet+Kyoto |access-date=2010-02-01}}</ref> In the beginning, archers shot arrows from the southern end of the veranda to the northern end where a [[:en:Curtain|Curtain]]-like ornament was erected as a [[:en:Bullseye (target)|target]]. The contest gained popularity during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and by the late 17th century competitions between participants from the [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū]] provinces were drawing big crowds.<ref name="panels" />
The duel between the famous warrior [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]] and Yoshioka Denshichirō, leader of the [[:en:Yoshioka-ryū|Yoshioka-ryū]], is popularly believed to have been fought just outside Sanjūsangen-dō in 1604.<ref name="panels" />
In the second Sunday of January, the temple has an event known as the ''Rite of the Willow'', where worshippers are touched on the head with a sacred willow branch to cure and prevent headaches, and a modern version of the ''Tōshiya'', the ''Festival of the Great Target'', is held on the west veranda, drawing roughly 2,000 participants from throughout Japan. Archers shoot arrows into targets approximately 50 - 100 centimeters in diameter and 60 meters (198 feet) away at the opposite end of the veranda. The festival was initially male-dominated, but in recent times it has shifted to being mainly women dressed in traditional [[:en:Kimono|kimonos]], with the target being 120 meters (394 feet) away.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="panels" /><ref name="JapanGuide" />
== Important features == The main deity of the temple is [[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']] or the Thousand Armed [[Kannon]]. The statue of the main deity was created by the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura]] [[:en:Sculpture|sculptor]] [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]] and is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. The temple also contains one thousand life-size statues of the Thousand Armed Kannon which stand on both the right and left sides of the main statue in 10 rows and 50 columns. Of these, 124 statues are from the original temple, rescued from the fire of 1249, while the remaining 876 statues were constructed in the 13th century. The statues are made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]] clad in gold leaf. The temple is 120 - meter long.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/kyoto/sanjusangen-do|title=Sanjusangen-do - Kyoto Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-08-16}}</ref> Around the 1000 Kannon statues stand 28 statues of guardian deities. There are also two famous statues of [[Fūjin]] and [[Raijin]].
===Guardian deities and Hinduism===
The 28 guardian deities stand in front of the Buddhist Kannon have their origins in Sanskrit texts of Hinduism. These ideas came to Japan through China, and the presence of both Hindu and Buddhist deities at Sanjūsangen-dō temple in Kyoto suggest various theories of the origin and spread of the spiritual and cultural ideas from India to east Asia.<ref name=wtg>Mark M. Jarzombek and Vikramaditya Prakash, A Global History of Architecture, 2nd Edition, Wiley, {{ISBN|978-0470402573}}</ref>
Life-size statues of these deities are housed at Sanjūsangen-dō where they guard the principal statue of the 11 feet tall seated Senju Kannon. The temple also features 1,000 standing statues of the Senju Kannon. The deities at Sanjūsangen-dō include Naraenkengo-ou, Misshaku-kongorikishi, Touhou-ten, Birurokusha-tennou, Birubakusha-tennou, Bishamonten, Daibon-tennou, Taishaku-ten, Daibenkudoku-ten, Mawara-ou, Jinmo-ten, Konpira-ou, Manzensha-ou, Hippakara-ou, Gobujyogo-ten, Konjikikujyaku-ou, Sanshitai-sho, Nandaryu-ou, Sakararyu-ou, Karura-ou, Kondai-ou, Mansen-ou, Magoraka-ou, Makeishura-ou, Kendabba-ou, Ashura-ou, Kinnara-ou and Basusennin. These deities trace their origins to Indian Dharmic mythology covering Hindu, Jain and Buddhist, and correspond to [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], [[:en:Vishnu|Vishnu]], [[:en:Lakshmi|Lakshmi]], [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], [[:en:Shiva|Shiva]], [[:en:Garuda|Garuda]], [[:en:Vayu|Vayu]], [[:en:Narayana|Narayana]], [[:en:Indra|Indra]] and others.<ref name=wtg/><ref>L. Chandra (1988), The Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara, {{ISBN|978-8170172475}}, Chapter 1</ref>
== Visitor Experience == Sanjūsangen-dō offers guided tours to visitors year-round. Visitors will get an in-depth look at the history of the temple as they explore its grand halls. Visitors can also come during the annual archery contest known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'', which showcases traditional archery skills. Visitors can get to the temple by taking a train ride to Kyoto's station then after a short bus ride on buses 206 and 208, followed by a 20-minute walk they will be at the temple. <ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref>
The temple is close to many other attractions, such as the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]] so visitors are encouraged to visit the other sights while at the temple. Cameras are not allowed inside the temple, but a photo book will be offered at the end of the guided tour that you can purchase.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Sanjusangendo Thousand-armed Kannon.JPG|Senju Kannon, the main statue of veneration in Sanjusangen-dō (by [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]]) File:Senju Kannon Sanjusangendo.jpg|Senju Kannon No.20 (by Tankei) File:Tokyo National Museum d004.jpg|Senju Kannon No.40 (by Tankei) File:Sanjusangendo twenty eight attendants.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Kendatsuba (乾闥婆) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Basu Sennin) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Basu Sennin (婆藪仙人) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Taishakuten) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Taishakuten (帝釈天)<ref>[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/28-bushu-kannon.shtml 28 Attendant Deities serving the 1,000-Armed Kannon] A-Z Photodictionary of Japanese Buddhist Statuary.</ref> File:Sanjusangendo Fujin old.jpg|[[Fūjin]], the Japanese [[kami]] of wind File:Sanjusangendo Raijin.jpg|[[Raijin]], the Japanese kami of thunder File:1363 Sanjiu Sangendo.jpg|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. The 28 attendants that once surrounded the central principal image are now relocated to the front of the 1000 statues of Senju Kannon. File:Kusakabe Kimbei 1364 Sanjiu.JPG|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. </gallery>

{{Nihongo|'''Sanjūsangen-dō'''|三十三間堂||''Temple of thirty-three [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]]''}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect in the [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama district]] of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The temple was founded in 1164 by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. It is officially known as {{nihongo|''Rengeō-in''|蓮華王院||''hall of the Lotus King''}} and belongs to the [[Myōhō-in]] temple complex.
Sanjūsangen-dō is most famous for its massively long ''hondō'' (main hall) dating from 1266 ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) and designated a [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure of Japan]], and the collection of sculptures it houses, including 1001 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]], 28 standing attendants, a statue of [[Fūjin]] and a statue of [[Raijin]], and the principal image of the temple, a big seated statue of Thousand-armed Kannon, all of them designated [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|National Treasures in the Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category of sculptures]], most of them dating to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] to [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]s.

== History == {{multiple image|perrow = 2 | image1 = Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png|width1=158 | image2 = Emperor Go-Shirakawa2.jpg|width2=158 | footer = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] }}
Sanjūsangen-dō was founded by the famous ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' and politician [[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118-1181) in 1164 for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].<ref name="Encyclopedia">{{cite web |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Sanjusangendo/ |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=[[World History Encyclopedia]] |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref><ref name="JapanGuide">{{cite web |url=https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3900.html |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=Japan Guide |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref> He built the temple in the emperor's own compound ''Hōjūji-dono'' in order to gain a noble title, that of Chancellor of the Realm, becoming the first ''samurai'' to do so. Go-Shirakawa's compound was around 1100 square meters in size, divided into ''Minamidono'' (the southern estate) and ''Kitadono'' (the northern estate). When Go-Shirakawa died in 1192, he was buried in the temple's east ''Hokkedō'' (hall of the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]]).<ref name="panels">Informative panels behind the main hall</ref>
The temple complex originally included several buildings other than the ''hondō'' ([[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]), including a ''gojūnotō'' (five-storied [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]]), a Kannondō (a hall of [[Kannon]]) and a Fudodō (a hall of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]).<ref name="panels" /> All of these buildings were completely destroyed in 1249 by a fire that broke out in the city.<ref name="Encyclopedia" /> The [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] (1220–1272) ordered the reconstruction of the ''hondō'', which began in 1251. The building was completed in 1266 and survives to the present day. From the original 1000 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]] dating from the temple's construction in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only 124 were saved from the fire. The Emperor also ordered 876 new Kannon statues to replace the lost ones. These were created by three groups of Buddhist sculptors, [[:en:Kei school|Kei school]] (Keiha), En school (Enpa) and In school (Inpa), during the course of 16 years.<ref name="panels"/>
A popular ''[[:en:Kyūjutsu|Kyūjutsu]]'' (archery) tournament known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'' ("passing arrow") was held at the west veranda of the temple for 255 years during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The contest originated in the late 16th century<ref>{{cite book |title=National Treasure Sanjūsangen-dō |location=[[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] |page=30 }}</ref> dating back to 1606 when a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named Asaoka Heibei is said to have shot 51 arrows in rapid succession down the length of the veranda.<ref>{{cite book |title=Lonely Planet Kyoto: City Guide |last=Rowthorn |first=Chris |year=2008 |publisher=Lonely Planet Publications |location=China |page=15 |isbn=9781740598453 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LR_Qj8x-iT4C&q=Lonely+Planet+Kyoto |access-date=2010-02-01}}</ref> In the beginning, archers shot arrows from the southern end of the veranda to the northern end where a [[:en:Curtain|Curtain]]-like ornament was erected as a [[:en:Bullseye (target)|target]]. The contest gained popularity during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and by the late 17th century competitions between participants from the [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū]] provinces were drawing big crowds.<ref name="panels" />
The duel between the famous warrior [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]] and Yoshioka Denshichirō, leader of the [[:en:Yoshioka-ryū|Yoshioka-ryū]], is popularly believed to have been fought just outside Sanjūsangen-dō in 1604.<ref name="panels" />
In the second Sunday of January, the temple has an event known as the ''Rite of the Willow'', where worshippers are touched on the head with a sacred willow branch to cure and prevent headaches, and a modern version of the ''Tōshiya'', the ''Festival of the Great Target'', is held on the west veranda, drawing roughly 2,000 participants from throughout Japan. Archers shoot arrows into targets approximately 50 - 100 centimeters in diameter and 60 meters (198 feet) away at the opposite end of the veranda. The festival was initially male-dominated, but in recent times it has shifted to being mainly women dressed in traditional [[:en:Kimono|kimonos]], with the target being 120 meters (394 feet) away.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="panels" /><ref name="JapanGuide" />
== Important features == The main deity of the temple is [[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']] or the Thousand Armed [[Kannon]]. The statue of the main deity was created by the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura]] [[:en:Sculpture|sculptor]] [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]] and is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. The temple also contains one thousand life-size statues of the Thousand Armed Kannon which stand on both the right and left sides of the main statue in 10 rows and 50 columns. Of these, 124 statues are from the original temple, rescued from the fire of 1249, while the remaining 876 statues were constructed in the 13th century. The statues are made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]] clad in gold leaf. The temple is 120 - meter long.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/kyoto/sanjusangen-do|title=Sanjusangen-do - Kyoto Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-08-16}}</ref> Around the 1000 Kannon statues stand 28 statues of guardian deities. There are also two famous statues of [[Fūjin]] and [[Raijin]].
===Guardian deities and Hinduism===
The 28 guardian deities stand in front of the Buddhist Kannon have their origins in Sanskrit texts of Hinduism. These ideas came to Japan through China, and the presence of both Hindu and Buddhist deities at Sanjūsangen-dō temple in Kyoto suggest various theories of the origin and spread of the spiritual and cultural ideas from India to east Asia.<ref name=wtg>Mark M. Jarzombek and Vikramaditya Prakash, A Global History of Architecture, 2nd Edition, Wiley, {{ISBN|978-0470402573}}</ref>
Life-size statues of these deities are housed at Sanjūsangen-dō where they guard the principal statue of the 11 feet tall seated Senju Kannon. The temple also features 1,000 standing statues of the Senju Kannon. The deities at Sanjūsangen-dō include Naraenkengo-ou, Misshaku-kongorikishi, Touhou-ten, Birurokusha-tennou, Birubakusha-tennou, Bishamonten, Daibon-tennou, Taishaku-ten, Daibenkudoku-ten, Mawara-ou, Jinmo-ten, Konpira-ou, Manzensha-ou, Hippakara-ou, Gobujyogo-ten, Konjikikujyaku-ou, Sanshitai-sho, Nandaryu-ou, Sakararyu-ou, Karura-ou, Kondai-ou, Mansen-ou, Magoraka-ou, Makeishura-ou, Kendabba-ou, Ashura-ou, Kinnara-ou and Basusennin. These deities trace their origins to Indian Dharmic mythology covering Hindu, Jain and Buddhist, and correspond to [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], [[:en:Vishnu|Vishnu]], [[:en:Lakshmi|Lakshmi]], [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], [[:en:Shiva|Shiva]], [[:en:Garuda|Garuda]], [[:en:Vayu|Vayu]], [[:en:Narayana|Narayana]], [[:en:Indra|Indra]] and others.<ref name=wtg/><ref>L. Chandra (1988), The Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara, {{ISBN|978-8170172475}}, Chapter 1</ref>
== Visitor Experience == Sanjūsangen-dō offers guided tours to visitors year-round. Visitors will get an in-depth look at the history of the temple as they explore its grand halls. Visitors can also come during the annual archery contest known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'', which showcases traditional archery skills. Visitors can get to the temple by taking a train ride to Kyoto's station then after a short bus ride on buses 206 and 208, followed by a 20-minute walk they will be at the temple. <ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref>
The temple is close to many other attractions, such as the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]] so visitors are encouraged to visit the other sights while at the temple. Cameras are not allowed inside the temple, but a photo book will be offered at the end of the guided tour that you can purchase.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Sanjusangendo Thousand-armed Kannon.JPG|Senju Kannon, the main statue of veneration in Sanjusangen-dō (by [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]]) File:Senju Kannon Sanjusangendo.jpg|Senju Kannon No.20 (by Tankei) File:Tokyo National Museum d004.jpg|Senju Kannon No.40 (by Tankei) File:Sanjusangendo twenty eight attendants.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Kendatsuba (乾闥婆) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Basu Sennin) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Basu Sennin (婆藪仙人) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Taishakuten) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Taishakuten (帝釈天)<ref>[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/28-bushu-kannon.shtml 28 Attendant Deities serving the 1,000-Armed Kannon] A-Z Photodictionary of Japanese Buddhist Statuary.</ref> File:Sanjusangendo Fujin old.jpg|[[Fūjin]], the Japanese [[kami]] of wind File:Sanjusangendo Raijin.jpg|[[Raijin]], the Japanese kami of thunder File:1363 Sanjiu Sangendo.jpg|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. The 28 attendants that once surrounded the central principal image are now relocated to the front of the 1000 statues of Senju Kannon. File:Kusakabe Kimbei 1364 Sanjiu.JPG|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. </gallery>

{{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|WD=Q281833}}|deity={{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q10904910}}|part_of={{ill|Myōhō-in|WD=Q10943054}}; {{ill|Q10380674|WD=Q10380674}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q653319]] Wikidata name: Sanjūsangen-dō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Sanjūsangen-dō | native_name = | image = Sanjusangendo 2022.jpg | image_size = 200px | alt = | caption = Sanjusangen-dō in 2022 | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 605-0941, Sanjusangendomawari, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] 675 | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = Thousand Armed [[Kannon]] ([[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']]) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] | year_completed = 1164 }}
{{Nihongo|'''Sanjūsangen-dō'''|三十三間堂||''Temple of thirty-three [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]]''}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect in the [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama district]] of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The temple was founded in 1164 by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. It is officially known as {{nihongo|''Rengeō-in''|蓮華王院||''hall of the Lotus King''}} and belongs to the [[Myōhō-in]] temple complex.
Sanjūsangen-dō is most famous for its massively long ''hondō'' (main hall) dating from 1266 ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) and designated a [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure of Japan]], and the collection of sculptures it houses, including 1001 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]], 28 standing attendants, a statue of [[Fūjin]] and a statue of [[Raijin]], and the principal image of the temple, a big seated statue of Thousand-armed Kannon, all of them designated [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|National Treasures in the Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category of sculptures]], most of them dating to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] to [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]s.
{{coord|34|59|16|N|135|46|18|E|display=title}}
== History == {{multiple image|perrow = 2 | image1 = Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png|width1=158 | image2 = Emperor Go-Shirakawa2.jpg|width2=158 | footer = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] }}

The temple also features 1,000 standing statues of the Senju Kannon. The deities at Sanjūsangen-dō include Naraenkengo-ou, Misshaku-kongorikishi, Touhou-ten, Birurokusha-tennou, Birubakusha-tennou, Bishamonten, Daibon-tennou, Taishaku-ten, Daibenkudoku-ten, Mawara-ou, Jinmo-ten, Konpira-ou, Manzensha-ou, Hippakara-ou, Gobujyogo-ten, Konjikikujyaku-ou, Sanshitai-sho, Nandaryu-ou, Sakararyu-ou, Karura-ou, Kondai-ou, Mansen-ou, Magoraka-ou, Makeishura-ou, Kendabba-ou, Ashura-ou, Kinnara-ou and Basusennin. These deities trace their origins to Indian Dharmic mythology covering Hindu, Jain and Buddhist, and correspond to [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], [[:en:Vishnu|Vishnu]], [[:en:Lakshmi|Lakshmi]], [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], [[:en:Shiva|Shiva]], [[:en:Garuda|Garuda]], [[:en:Vayu|Vayu]], [[:en:Narayana|Narayana]], [[:en:Indra|Indra]] and others.<ref name=wtg/><ref>L. Chandra (1988), The Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara, {{ISBN|978-8170172475}}, Chapter 1</ref>
== Visitor Experience == Sanjūsangen-dō offers guided tours to visitors year-round. Visitors will get an in-depth look at the history of the temple as they explore its grand halls. Visitors can also come during the annual archery contest known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'', which showcases traditional archery skills. Visitors can get to the temple by taking a train ride to Kyoto's station then after a short bus ride on buses 206 and 208, followed by a 20-minute walk they will be at the temple. <ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref>
The temple is close to many other attractions, such as the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]] so visitors are encouraged to visit the other sights while at the temple. Cameras are not allowed inside the temple, but a photo book will be offered at the end of the guided tour that you can purchase.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Sanjusangendo Thousand-armed Kannon.JPG|Senju Kannon, the main statue of veneration in Sanjusangen-dō (by [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]]) File:Senju Kannon Sanjusangendo.jpg|Senju Kannon No.20 (by Tankei) File:Tokyo National Museum d004.jpg|Senju Kannon No.40 (by Tankei) File:Sanjusangendo twenty eight attendants.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Kendatsuba (乾闥婆) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Basu Sennin) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Basu Sennin (婆藪仙人) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Taishakuten) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Taishakuten (帝釈天)<ref>[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/28-bushu-kannon.shtml 28 Attendant Deities serving the 1,000-Armed Kannon] A-Z Photodictionary of Japanese Buddhist Statuary.</ref> File:Sanjusangendo Fujin old.jpg|[[Fūjin]], the Japanese [[kami]] of wind File:Sanjusangendo Raijin.jpg|[[Raijin]], the Japanese kami of thunder File:1363 Sanjiu Sangendo.jpg|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. The 28 attendants that once surrounded the central principal image are now relocated to the front of the 1000 statues of Senju Kannon. File:Kusakabe Kimbei 1364 Sanjiu.JPG|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. </gallery>

{{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|WD=Q281833}}|deity={{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q10904910}}|part_of={{ill|Myōhō-in|WD=Q10943054}}; {{ill|Q10380674|WD=Q10380674}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q653319]] Wikidata name: Sanjūsangen-dō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Sanjūsangen-dō | native_name = | image = Sanjusangendo 2022.jpg | image_size = 200px | alt = | caption = Sanjusangen-dō in 2022 | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 605-0941, Sanjusangendomawari, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] 675 | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = Thousand Armed [[Kannon]] ([[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']]) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] | year_completed = 1164 }}
{{Nihongo|'''Sanjūsangen-dō'''|三十三間堂||''Temple of thirty-three [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]]''}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect in the [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama district]] of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The temple was founded in 1164 by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. It is officially known as {{nihongo|''Rengeō-in''|蓮華王院||''hall of the Lotus King''}} and belongs to the [[Myōhō-in]] temple complex.
Sanjūsangen-dō is most famous for its massively long ''hondō'' (main hall) dating from 1266 ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) and designated a [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure of Japan]], and the collection of sculptures it houses, including 1001 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]], 28 standing attendants, a statue of [[Fūjin]] and a statue of [[Raijin]], and the principal image of the temple, a big seated statue of Thousand-armed Kannon, all of them designated [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|National Treasures in the Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category of sculptures]], most of them dating to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] to [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]s.
{{coord|34|59|16|N|135|46|18|E|display=title}}
== History == {{multiple image|perrow = 2 | image1 = Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png|width1=158 | image2 = Emperor Go-Shirakawa2.jpg|width2=158 | footer = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] }}
Sanjūsangen-dō was founded by the famous ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' and politician [[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118-1181) in 1164 for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].<ref name="Encyclopedia">{{cite web |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Sanjusangendo/ |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=[[World History Encyclopedia]] |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref><ref name="JapanGuide">{{cite web |url=https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3900.html |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=Japan Guide |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref> He built the temple in the emperor's own compound ''Hōjūji-dono'' in order to gain a noble title, that of Chancellor of the Realm, becoming the first ''samurai'' to do so. Go-Shirakawa's compound was around 1100 square meters in size, divided into ''Minamidono'' (the southern estate) and ''Kitadono'' (the northern estate). When Go-Shirakawa died in 1192, he was buried in the temple's east ''Hokkedō'' (hall of the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]]).<ref name="panels">Informative panels behind the main hall</ref>
The temple complex originally included several buildings other than the ''hondō'' ([[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]), including a ''gojūnotō'' (five-storied [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]]), a Kannondō (a hall of [[Kannon]]) and a Fudodō (a hall of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]).<ref name="panels" /> All of these buildings were completely destroyed in 1249 by a fire that broke out in the city.<ref name="Encyclopedia" /> The [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] (1220–1272) ordered the reconstruction of the ''hondō'', which began in 1251. The building was completed in 1266 and survives to the present day. From the original 1000 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]] dating from the temple's construction in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only 124 were saved from the fire. The Emperor also ordered 876 new Kannon statues to replace the lost ones. These were created by three groups of Buddhist sculptors, [[:en:Kei school|Kei school]] (Keiha), En school (Enpa) and In school (Inpa), during the course of 16 years.<ref name="panels"/>
A popular ''[[:en:Kyūjutsu|Kyūjutsu]]'' (archery) tournament known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'' ("passing arrow") was held at the west veranda of the temple for 255 years during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The contest originated in the late 16th century<ref>{{cite book |title=National Treasure Sanjūsangen-dō |location=[[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] |page=30 }}</ref> dating back to 1606 when a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named Asaoka Heibei is said to have shot 51 arrows in rapid succession down the length of the veranda.<ref>{{cite book |title=Lonely Planet Kyoto: City Guide |last=Rowthorn |first=Chris |year=2008 |publisher=Lonely Planet Publications |location=China |page=15 |isbn=9781740598453 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LR_Qj8x-iT4C&q=Lonely+Planet+Kyoto |access-date=2010-02-01}}</ref> In the beginning, archers shot arrows from the southern end of the veranda to the northern end where a [[:en:Curtain|Curtain]]-like ornament was erected as a [[:en:Bullseye (target)|target]]. The contest gained popularity during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and by the late 17th century competitions between participants from the [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū]] provinces were drawing big crowds.<ref name="panels" />
The duel between the famous warrior [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]] and Yoshioka Denshichirō, leader of the [[:en:Yoshioka-ryū|Yoshioka-ryū]], is popularly believed to have been fought just outside Sanjūsangen-dō in 1604.<ref name="panels" />
In the second Sunday of January, the temple has an event known as the ''Rite of the Willow'', where worshippers are touched on the head with a sacred willow branch to cure and prevent headaches, and a modern version of the ''Tōshiya'', the ''Festival of the Great Target'', is held on the west veranda, drawing roughly 2,000 participants from throughout Japan. Archers shoot arrows into targets approximately 50 - 100 centimeters in diameter and 60 meters (198 feet) away at the opposite end of the veranda. The festival was initially male-dominated, but in recent times it has shifted to being mainly women dressed in traditional [[:en:Kimono|kimonos]], with the target being 120 meters (394 feet) away.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="panels" /><ref name="JapanGuide" />
== Important features == The main deity of the temple is [[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']] or the Thousand Armed [[Kannon]]. The statue of the main deity was created by the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura]] [[:en:Sculpture|sculptor]] [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]] and is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. The temple also contains one thousand life-size statues of the Thousand Armed Kannon which stand on both the right and left sides of the main statue in 10 rows and 50 columns. Of these, 124 statues are from the original temple, rescued from the fire of 1249, while the remaining 876 statues were constructed in the 13th century. The statues are made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]] clad in gold leaf. The temple is 120 - meter long.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/kyoto/sanjusangen-do|title=Sanjusangen-do - Kyoto Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-08-16}}</ref> Around the 1000 Kannon statues stand 28 statues of guardian deities. There are also two famous statues of [[Fūjin]] and [[Raijin]].
===Guardian deities and Hinduism===
The 28 guardian deities stand in front of the Buddhist Kannon have their origins in Sanskrit texts of Hinduism. These ideas came to Japan through China, and the presence of both Hindu and Buddhist deities at Sanjūsangen-dō temple in Kyoto suggest various theories of the origin and spread of the spiritual and cultural ideas from India to east Asia.<ref name=wtg>Mark M. Jarzombek and Vikramaditya Prakash, A Global History of Architecture, 2nd Edition, Wiley, {{ISBN|978-0470402573}}</ref>
Life-size statues of these deities are housed at Sanjūsangen-dō where they guard the principal statue of the 11 feet tall seated Senju Kannon. The temple also features 1,000 standing statues of the Senju Kannon. The deities at Sanjūsangen-dō include Naraenkengo-ou, Misshaku-kongorikishi, Touhou-ten, Birurokusha-tennou, Birubakusha-tennou, Bishamonten, Daibon-tennou, Taishaku-ten, Daibenkudoku-ten, Mawara-ou, Jinmo-ten, Konpira-ou, Manzensha-ou, Hippakara-ou, Gobujyogo-ten, Konjikikujyaku-ou, Sanshitai-sho, Nandaryu-ou, Sakararyu-ou, Karura-ou, Kondai-ou, Mansen-ou, Magoraka-ou, Makeishura-ou, Kendabba-ou, Ashura-ou, Kinnara-ou and Basusennin. These deities trace their origins to Indian Dharmic mythology covering Hindu, Jain and Buddhist, and correspond to [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], [[:en:Vishnu|Vishnu]], [[:en:Lakshmi|Lakshmi]], [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], [[:en:Shiva|Shiva]], [[:en:Garuda|Garuda]], [[:en:Vayu|Vayu]], [[:en:Narayana|Narayana]], [[:en:Indra|Indra]] and others.<ref name=wtg/><ref>L. Chandra (1988), The Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara, {{ISBN|978-8170172475}}, Chapter 1</ref>
== Visitor Experience == Sanjūsangen-dō offers guided tours to visitors year-round. Visitors will get an in-depth look at the history of the temple as they explore its grand halls. Visitors can also come during the annual archery contest known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'', which showcases traditional archery skills. Visitors can get to the temple by taking a train ride to Kyoto's station then after a short bus ride on buses 206 and 208, followed by a 20-minute walk they will be at the temple. <ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref>
The temple is close to many other attractions, such as the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]] so visitors are encouraged to visit the other sights while at the temple. Cameras are not allowed inside the temple, but a photo book will be offered at the end of the guided tour that you can purchase.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Sanjusangendo Thousand-armed Kannon.JPG|Senju Kannon, the main statue of veneration in Sanjusangen-dō (by [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]]) File:Senju Kannon Sanjusangendo.jpg|Senju Kannon No.20 (by Tankei) File:Tokyo National Museum d004.jpg|Senju Kannon No.40 (by Tankei) File:Sanjusangendo twenty eight attendants.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Kendatsuba (乾闥婆) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Basu Sennin) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Basu Sennin (婆藪仙人) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Taishakuten) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Taishakuten (帝釈天)<ref>[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/28-bushu-kannon.shtml 28 Attendant Deities serving the 1,000-Armed Kannon] A-Z Photodictionary of Japanese Buddhist Statuary.</ref> File:Sanjusangendo Fujin old.jpg|[[Fūjin]], the Japanese [[kami]] of wind File:Sanjusangendo Raijin.jpg|[[Raijin]], the Japanese kami of thunder File:1363 Sanjiu Sangendo.jpg|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. The 28 attendants that once surrounded the central principal image are now relocated to the front of the 1000 statues of Senju Kannon. File:Kusakabe Kimbei 1364 Sanjiu.JPG|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. </gallery>

{{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|WD=Q281833}}|deity={{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q10904910}}|part_of={{ill|Myōhō-in|WD=Q10943054}}; {{ill|Q10380674|WD=Q10380674}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q653319]] Wikidata name: Sanjūsangen-dō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Sanjūsangen-dō | native_name = | image = Sanjusangendo 2022.jpg | image_size = 200px | alt = | caption = Sanjusangen-dō in 2022 | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 605-0941, Sanjusangendomawari, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] 675 | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = Thousand Armed [[Kannon]] ([[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']]) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] | year_completed = 1164 }}
{{Nihongo|'''Sanjūsangen-dō'''|三十三間堂||''Temple of thirty-three [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]]''}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect in the [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama district]] of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The temple was founded in 1164 by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. It is officially known as {{nihongo|''Rengeō-in''|蓮華王院||''hall of the Lotus King''}} and belongs to the [[Myōhō-in]] temple complex.
Sanjūsangen-dō is most famous for its massively long ''hondō'' (main hall) dating from 1266 ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) and designated a [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure of Japan]], and the collection of sculptures it houses, including 1001 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]], 28 standing attendants, a statue of [[Fūjin]] and a statue of [[Raijin]], and the principal image of the temple, a big seated statue of Thousand-armed Kannon, all of them designated [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|National Treasures in the Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category of sculptures]], most of them dating to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] to [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]s.
{{coord|34|59|16|N|135|46|18|E|display=title}}
== History == {{multiple image|perrow = 2 | image1 = Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png|width1=158 | image2 = Emperor Go-Shirakawa2.jpg|width2=158 | footer = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] }}
Sanjūsangen-dō was founded by the famous ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' and politician [[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118-1181) in 1164 for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].<ref name="Encyclopedia">{{cite web |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Sanjusangendo/ |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=[[World History Encyclopedia]] |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref><ref name="JapanGuide">{{cite web |url=https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3900.html |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=Japan Guide |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref> He built the temple in the emperor's own compound ''Hōjūji-dono'' in order to gain a noble title, that of Chancellor of the Realm, becoming the first ''samurai'' to do so. Go-Shirakawa's compound was around 1100 square meters in size, divided into ''Minamidono'' (the southern estate) and ''Kitadono'' (the northern estate). When Go-Shirakawa died in 1192, he was buried in the temple's east ''Hokkedō'' (hall of the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]]).<ref name="panels">Informative panels behind the main hall</ref>
The temple complex originally included several buildings other than the ''hondō'' ([[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]), including a ''gojūnotō'' (five-storied [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]]), a Kannondō (a hall of [[Kannon]]) and a Fudodō (a hall of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]).<ref name="panels" /> All of these buildings were completely destroyed in 1249 by a fire that broke out in the city.<ref name="Encyclopedia" /> The [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] (1220–1272) ordered the reconstruction of the ''hondō'', which began in 1251. The building was completed in 1266 and survives to the present day. From the original 1000 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]] dating from the temple's construction in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only 124 were saved from the fire. The Emperor also ordered 876 new Kannon statues to replace the lost ones. These were created by three groups of Buddhist sculptors, [[:en:Kei school|Kei school]] (Keiha), En school (Enpa) and In school (Inpa), during the course of 16 years.<ref name="panels"/>
A popular ''[[:en:Kyūjutsu|Kyūjutsu]]'' (archery) tournament known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'' ("passing arrow") was held at the west veranda of the temple for 255 years during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The contest originated in the late 16th century<ref>{{cite book |title=National Treasure Sanjūsangen-dō |location=[[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] |page=30 }}</ref> dating back to 1606 when a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named Asaoka Heibei is said to have shot 51 arrows in rapid succession down the length of the veranda.<ref>{{cite book |title=Lonely Planet Kyoto: City Guide |last=Rowthorn |first=Chris |year=2008 |publisher=Lonely Planet Publications |location=China |page=15 |isbn=9781740598453 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LR_Qj8x-iT4C&q=Lonely+Planet+Kyoto |access-date=2010-02-01}}</ref> In the beginning, archers shot arrows from the southern end of the veranda to the northern end where a [[:en:Curtain|Curtain]]-like ornament was erected as a [[:en:Bullseye (target)|target]]. The contest gained popularity during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and by the late 17th century competitions between participants from the [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū]] provinces were drawing big crowds.<ref name="panels" />
The duel between the famous warrior [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]] and Yoshioka Denshichirō, leader of the [[:en:Yoshioka-ryū|Yoshioka-ryū]], is popularly believed to have been fought just outside Sanjūsangen-dō in 1604.<ref name="panels" />
In the second Sunday of January, the temple has an event known as the ''Rite of the Willow'', where worshippers are touched on the head with a sacred willow branch to cure and prevent headaches, and a modern version of the ''Tōshiya'', the ''Festival of the Great Target'', is held on the west veranda, drawing roughly 2,000 participants from throughout Japan. Archers shoot arrows into targets approximately 50 - 100 centimeters in diameter and 60 meters (198 feet) away at the opposite end of the veranda. The festival was initially male-dominated, but in recent times it has shifted to being mainly women dressed in traditional [[:en:Kimono|kimonos]], with the target being 120 meters (394 feet) away.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="panels" /><ref name="JapanGuide" />
== Important features == The main deity of the temple is [[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']] or the Thousand Armed [[Kannon]]. The statue of the main deity was created by the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura]] [[:en:Sculpture|sculptor]] [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]] and is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. The temple also contains one thousand life-size statues of the Thousand Armed Kannon which stand on both the right and left sides of the main statue in 10 rows and 50 columns. Of these, 124 statues are from the original temple, rescued from the fire of 1249, while the remaining 876 statues were constructed in the 13th century. The statues are made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]] clad in gold leaf. The temple is 120 - meter long.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/kyoto/sanjusangen-do|title=Sanjusangen-do - Kyoto Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-08-16}}</ref> Around the 1000 Kannon statues stand 28 statues of guardian deities. There are also two famous statues of [[Fūjin]] and [[Raijin]].
===Guardian deities and Hinduism===
The 28 guardian deities stand in front of the Buddhist Kannon have their origins in Sanskrit texts of Hinduism. These ideas came to Japan through China, and the presence of both Hindu and Buddhist deities at Sanjūsangen-dō temple in Kyoto suggest various theories of the origin and spread of the spiritual and cultural ideas from India to east Asia.<ref name=wtg>Mark M. Jarzombek and Vikramaditya Prakash, A Global History of Architecture, 2nd Edition, Wiley, {{ISBN|978-0470402573}}</ref>
Life-size statues of these deities are housed at Sanjūsangen-dō where they guard the principal statue of the 11 feet tall seated Senju Kannon. The temple also features 1,000 standing statues of the Senju Kannon. The deities at Sanjūsangen-dō include Naraenkengo-ou, Misshaku-kongorikishi, Touhou-ten, Birurokusha-tennou, Birubakusha-tennou, Bishamonten, Daibon-tennou, Taishaku-ten, Daibenkudoku-ten, Mawara-ou, Jinmo-ten, Konpira-ou, Manzensha-ou, Hippakara-ou, Gobujyogo-ten, Konjikikujyaku-ou, Sanshitai-sho, Nandaryu-ou, Sakararyu-ou, Karura-ou, Kondai-ou, Mansen-ou, Magoraka-ou, Makeishura-ou, Kendabba-ou, Ashura-ou, Kinnara-ou and Basusennin. These deities trace their origins to Indian Dharmic mythology covering Hindu, Jain and Buddhist, and correspond to [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], [[:en:Vishnu|Vishnu]], [[:en:Lakshmi|Lakshmi]], [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], [[:en:Shiva|Shiva]], [[:en:Garuda|Garuda]], [[:en:Vayu|Vayu]], [[:en:Narayana|Narayana]], [[:en:Indra|Indra]] and others.<ref name=wtg/><ref>L. Chandra (1988), The Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara, {{ISBN|978-8170172475}}, Chapter 1</ref>
== Visitor Experience == Sanjūsangen-dō offers guided tours to visitors year-round. Visitors will get an in-depth look at the history of the temple as they explore its grand halls. Visitors can also come during the annual archery contest known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'', which showcases traditional archery skills. Visitors can get to the temple by taking a train ride to Kyoto's station then after a short bus ride on buses 206 and 208, followed by a 20-minute walk they will be at the temple. <ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref>
The temple is close to many other attractions, such as the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]] so visitors are encouraged to visit the other sights while at the temple. Cameras are not allowed inside the temple, but a photo book will be offered at the end of the guided tour that you can purchase.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Sanjusangendo Thousand-armed Kannon.JPG|Senju Kannon, the main statue of veneration in Sanjusangen-dō (by [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]]) File:Senju Kannon Sanjusangendo.jpg|Senju Kannon No.20 (by Tankei) File:Tokyo National Museum d004.jpg|Senju Kannon No.40 (by Tankei) File:Sanjusangendo twenty eight attendants.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Kendatsuba (乾闥婆) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Basu Sennin) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Basu Sennin (婆藪仙人) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Taishakuten) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Taishakuten (帝釈天)<ref>[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/28-bushu-kannon.shtml 28 Attendant Deities serving the 1,000-Armed Kannon] A-Z Photodictionary of Japanese Buddhist Statuary.</ref> File:Sanjusangendo Fujin old.jpg|[[Fūjin]], the Japanese [[kami]] of wind File:Sanjusangendo Raijin.jpg|[[Raijin]], the Japanese kami of thunder File:1363 Sanjiu Sangendo.jpg|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. The 28 attendants that once surrounded the central principal image are now relocated to the front of the 1000 statues of Senju Kannon. File:Kusakabe Kimbei 1364 Sanjiu.JPG|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. </gallery>

{{Nihongo|'''Sanjūsangen-dō'''|三十三間堂||''Temple of thirty-three [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]]''}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect in the [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama district]] of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The temple was founded in 1164 by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. It is officially known as {{nihongo|''Rengeō-in''|蓮華王院||''hall of the Lotus King''}} and belongs to the [[Myōhō-in]] temple complex.
Sanjūsangen-dō is most famous for its massively long ''hondō'' (main hall) dating from 1266 ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) and designated a [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure of Japan]], and the collection of sculptures it houses, including 1001 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]], 28 standing attendants, a statue of [[Fūjin]] and a statue of [[Raijin]], and the principal image of the temple, a big seated statue of Thousand-armed Kannon, all of them designated [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|National Treasures in the Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category of sculptures]], most of them dating to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] to [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]s.

== History == {{multiple image|perrow = 2 | image1 = Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png|width1=158 | image2 = Emperor Go-Shirakawa2.jpg|width2=158 | footer = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] }}
Sanjūsangen-dō was founded by the famous ''[[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]'' and politician [[Taira no Kiyomori]] (1118-1181) in 1164 for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]].<ref name="Encyclopedia">{{cite web |url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Sanjusangendo/ |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=[[World History Encyclopedia]] |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref><ref name="JapanGuide">{{cite web |url=https://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3900.html |title=Sanjusangendo |author= |publisher=Japan Guide |access-date=2019-02-18 }}</ref> He built the temple in the emperor's own compound ''Hōjūji-dono'' in order to gain a noble title, that of Chancellor of the Realm, becoming the first ''samurai'' to do so. Go-Shirakawa's compound was around 1100 square meters in size, divided into ''Minamidono'' (the southern estate) and ''Kitadono'' (the northern estate). When Go-Shirakawa died in 1192, he was buried in the temple's east ''Hokkedō'' (hall of the [[:en:en:Lotus Sutra|en:Lotus Sutra]]).<ref name="panels">Informative panels behind the main hall</ref>
The temple complex originally included several buildings other than the ''hondō'' ([[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]), including a ''gojūnotō'' (five-storied [[:en:Pagoda|Pagoda]]), a Kannondō (a hall of [[Kannon]]) and a Fudodō (a hall of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]]).<ref name="panels" /> All of these buildings were completely destroyed in 1249 by a fire that broke out in the city.<ref name="Encyclopedia" /> The [[:en:Emperor Go-Saga|Emperor Go-Saga]] (1220–1272) ordered the reconstruction of the ''hondō'', which began in 1251. The building was completed in 1266 and survives to the present day. From the original 1000 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]] dating from the temple's construction in the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], only 124 were saved from the fire. The Emperor also ordered 876 new Kannon statues to replace the lost ones. These were created by three groups of Buddhist sculptors, [[:en:Kei school|Kei school]] (Keiha), En school (Enpa) and In school (Inpa), during the course of 16 years.<ref name="panels"/>
A popular ''[[:en:Kyūjutsu|Kyūjutsu]]'' (archery) tournament known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'' ("passing arrow") was held at the west veranda of the temple for 255 years during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. The contest originated in the late 16th century<ref>{{cite book |title=National Treasure Sanjūsangen-dō |location=[[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] |page=30 }}</ref> dating back to 1606 when a [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] named Asaoka Heibei is said to have shot 51 arrows in rapid succession down the length of the veranda.<ref>{{cite book |title=Lonely Planet Kyoto: City Guide |last=Rowthorn |first=Chris |year=2008 |publisher=Lonely Planet Publications |location=China |page=15 |isbn=9781740598453 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LR_Qj8x-iT4C&q=Lonely+Planet+Kyoto |access-date=2010-02-01}}</ref> In the beginning, archers shot arrows from the southern end of the veranda to the northern end where a [[:en:Curtain|Curtain]]-like ornament was erected as a [[:en:Bullseye (target)|target]]. The contest gained popularity during the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] and by the late 17th century competitions between participants from the [[:en:Owari Province|Owari]] and [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishū]] provinces were drawing big crowds.<ref name="panels" />
The duel between the famous warrior [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]] and Yoshioka Denshichirō, leader of the [[:en:Yoshioka-ryū|Yoshioka-ryū]], is popularly believed to have been fought just outside Sanjūsangen-dō in 1604.<ref name="panels" />
In the second Sunday of January, the temple has an event known as the ''Rite of the Willow'', where worshippers are touched on the head with a sacred willow branch to cure and prevent headaches, and a modern version of the ''Tōshiya'', the ''Festival of the Great Target'', is held on the west veranda, drawing roughly 2,000 participants from throughout Japan. Archers shoot arrows into targets approximately 50 - 100 centimeters in diameter and 60 meters (198 feet) away at the opposite end of the veranda. The festival was initially male-dominated, but in recent times it has shifted to being mainly women dressed in traditional [[:en:Kimono|kimonos]], with the target being 120 meters (394 feet) away.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="panels" /><ref name="JapanGuide" />
== Important features == The main deity of the temple is [[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']] or the Thousand Armed [[Kannon]]. The statue of the main deity was created by the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura]] [[:en:Sculpture|sculptor]] [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]] and is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure of Japan]]. The temple also contains one thousand life-size statues of the Thousand Armed Kannon which stand on both the right and left sides of the main statue in 10 rows and 50 columns. Of these, 124 statues are from the original temple, rescued from the fire of 1249, while the remaining 876 statues were constructed in the 13th century. The statues are made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]] clad in gold leaf. The temple is 120 - meter long.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/kyoto/sanjusangen-do|title=Sanjusangen-do - Kyoto Travel Guide {{!}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-08-16}}</ref> Around the 1000 Kannon statues stand 28 statues of guardian deities. There are also two famous statues of [[Fūjin]] and [[Raijin]].
===Guardian deities and Hinduism===
The 28 guardian deities stand in front of the Buddhist Kannon have their origins in Sanskrit texts of Hinduism. These ideas came to Japan through China, and the presence of both Hindu and Buddhist deities at Sanjūsangen-dō temple in Kyoto suggest various theories of the origin and spread of the spiritual and cultural ideas from India to east Asia.<ref name=wtg>Mark M. Jarzombek and Vikramaditya Prakash, A Global History of Architecture, 2nd Edition, Wiley, {{ISBN|978-0470402573}}</ref>
Life-size statues of these deities are housed at Sanjūsangen-dō where they guard the principal statue of the 11 feet tall seated Senju Kannon. The temple also features 1,000 standing statues of the Senju Kannon. The deities at Sanjūsangen-dō include Naraenkengo-ou, Misshaku-kongorikishi, Touhou-ten, Birurokusha-tennou, Birubakusha-tennou, Bishamonten, Daibon-tennou, Taishaku-ten, Daibenkudoku-ten, Mawara-ou, Jinmo-ten, Konpira-ou, Manzensha-ou, Hippakara-ou, Gobujyogo-ten, Konjikikujyaku-ou, Sanshitai-sho, Nandaryu-ou, Sakararyu-ou, Karura-ou, Kondai-ou, Mansen-ou, Magoraka-ou, Makeishura-ou, Kendabba-ou, Ashura-ou, Kinnara-ou and Basusennin. These deities trace their origins to Indian Dharmic mythology covering Hindu, Jain and Buddhist, and correspond to [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], [[:en:Vishnu|Vishnu]], [[:en:Lakshmi|Lakshmi]], [[:en:Brahma|Brahma]], [[:en:Shiva|Shiva]], [[:en:Garuda|Garuda]], [[:en:Vayu|Vayu]], [[:en:Narayana|Narayana]], [[:en:Indra|Indra]] and others.<ref name=wtg/><ref>L. Chandra (1988), The Thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara, {{ISBN|978-8170172475}}, Chapter 1</ref>
== Visitor Experience == Sanjūsangen-dō offers guided tours to visitors year-round. Visitors will get an in-depth look at the history of the temple as they explore its grand halls. Visitors can also come during the annual archery contest known as ''[[:en:Tōshiya|Tōshiya]]'', which showcases traditional archery skills. Visitors can get to the temple by taking a train ride to Kyoto's station then after a short bus ride on buses 206 and 208, followed by a 20-minute walk they will be at the temple. <ref>{{Cite web |last=Precy |date=2023-08-25 |title=Sanjusangen-do Temple: Kyoto's Hall of a Thousand Kannons |url=https://japaninsides.com/sanjusangen-do-temple-35159 |access-date=2024-11-22 |website=japaninsides.com |language=en-US}}</ref>
The temple is close to many other attractions, such as the [[:en:Kyoto National Museum|Kyoto National Museum]] so visitors are encouraged to visit the other sights while at the temple. Cameras are not allowed inside the temple, but a photo book will be offered at the end of the guided tour that you can purchase.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Sanjusangendo Thousand-armed Kannon.JPG|Senju Kannon, the main statue of veneration in Sanjusangen-dō (by [[:en:Tankei|Tankei]]) File:Senju Kannon Sanjusangendo.jpg|Senju Kannon No.20 (by Tankei) File:Tokyo National Museum d004.jpg|Senju Kannon No.40 (by Tankei) File:Sanjusangendo twenty eight attendants.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Kendatsuba (乾闥婆) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Basu Sennin) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Basu Sennin (婆藪仙人) File:Twenty-Eight Attendants (Taishakuten) Sanjusangendo.jpg|One of the 28 Sanjusangen-do temple deities, Taishakuten (帝釈天)<ref>[http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/28-bushu-kannon.shtml 28 Attendant Deities serving the 1,000-Armed Kannon] A-Z Photodictionary of Japanese Buddhist Statuary.</ref> File:Sanjusangendo Fujin old.jpg|[[Fūjin]], the Japanese [[kami]] of wind File:Sanjusangendo Raijin.jpg|[[Raijin]], the Japanese kami of thunder File:1363 Sanjiu Sangendo.jpg|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. The 28 attendants that once surrounded the central principal image are now relocated to the front of the 1000 statues of Senju Kannon. File:Kusakabe Kimbei 1364 Sanjiu.JPG|The arrangement of Buddha statues in the past. </gallery>

{{ill|Taira no Kiyomori|WD=Q281833}}|deity={{ill|Thousand-Armed Thousand-Eyed Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q10904910}}|part_of={{ill|Myōhō-in|WD=Q10943054}}; {{ill|Q10380674|WD=Q10380674}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q653319]] Wikidata name: Sanjūsangen-dō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Kyoto, Japan}}{{Expand Japanese|topic=struct}}{{Infobox religious building | name = Sanjūsangen-dō | native_name = | image = Sanjusangendo 2022.jpg | image_size = 200px | alt = | caption = Sanjusangen-dō in 2022 | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 605-0941, Sanjusangendomawari, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] 675 | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = Thousand Armed [[Kannon]] ([[:en:Avalokitesvara|''Thousand-armed Kannon-arya-avalokiteśvara'']]) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] | year_completed = 1164 }}
{{Nihongo|'''Sanjūsangen-dō'''|三十三間堂||''Temple of thirty-three [[:en:Ken (unit)|bays]]''}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] of the [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] sect in the [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama district]] of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
The temple was founded in 1164 by [[Taira no Kiyomori]] for the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. It is officially known as {{nihongo|''Rengeō-in''|蓮華王院||''hall of the Lotus King''}} and belongs to the [[Myōhō-in]] temple complex.
Sanjūsangen-dō is most famous for its massively long ''hondō'' (main hall) dating from 1266 ([[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]) and designated a [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|National Treasure of Japan]], and the collection of sculptures it houses, including 1001 standing [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Thousand-armed Kannon]], 28 standing attendants, a statue of [[Fūjin]] and a statue of [[Raijin]], and the principal image of the temple, a big seated statue of Thousand-armed Kannon, all of them designated [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|National Treasures in the Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category of sculptures]], most of them dating to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian]] to [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]]s.
{{coord|34|59|16|N|135|46|18|E|display=title}}
== History == {{multiple image|perrow = 2 | image1 = Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png|width1=158 | image2 = Emperor Go-Shirakawa2.jpg|width2=158 | footer = [[Taira no Kiyomori]] and [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]] }}
